title: Foxfire Interview Ethics: Community as Knowledge-Holder
description: Explore the core ethical principles of the Foxfire approach to community documentation. Learn how Foxfire students treated community elders as authoritative experts, how they prepared for and conducted interviews, and what ethical obligations arise when documenting living community knowledge.

steps.instruction: Read about the Foxfire project origin. Why did Wigginton frame community elders as the equals of textbook authors in knowledge?
steps.culturalContext: The Foxfire project began in 1966 when high school teacher Eliot Wigginton challenged his students in Rabun Gap, Georgia, to document the living knowledge of their Appalachian community rather than use a standard English textbook. Students conducted interviews with elders, published the results in a magazine called Foxfire, and created what became one of the most important records of Appalachian heritage knowledge in existence. The Foxfire approach reversed the usual educational hierarchy: instead of students receiving knowledge from textbooks authored by distant experts, students went into their communities and treated local elders as the experts. This was a radical reframing of who holds legitimate knowledge — and it produced 12 volumes of Appalachian heritage documentation that libraries and universities still use today.

steps.instruction: Draft a brief introduction you would give to an elder before asking to interview them about a heritage skill. Include: who you are, why you want to know, what you will do with the information, and how they can say no or set limits.
steps.culturalContext: The ethics of asking: before interviewing anyone, you must ask permission. Explain who you are, why you are doing this, what you will do with the information, and who will see it. The community member has the right to say no, to limit what is shared, and to review your work before it is published. The Foxfire students learned that community members were willing to share extraordinary depth of knowledge when they trusted the interviewer's intentions and felt their expertise was genuinely valued. Trust is not assumed — it is built through respectful introduction and demonstrated through how you handle what you are given.

steps.instruction: Practice rewriting these leading questions as open questions: (a) 'Did your grandmother teach you to quilt?' (b) 'Is log cabin construction difficult?' (c) 'Do you think young people have forgotten these skills?'
steps.culturalContext: Listening without assumptions: a good heritage interview follows the subject, not the interviewer's agenda. Ask open questions ('Tell me about how you...') rather than leading questions ('Is it true that you...'). Let silences sit — sometimes the best material follows a pause. Take notes on what surprises you. When you rewrite a leading question as an open question, you create space for the community member's actual experience rather than confirming what you already thought you knew.

steps.instruction: Imagine you have just heard an elder describe how to select a tree for a canoe frame. Write three things you would note beyond the technical instructions — context, attitude, associated knowledge.
steps.culturalContext: Documenting what you hear: write down the speaker's own words as accurately as possible. Do not paraphrase in ways that change the meaning. If you are not sure what someone meant, ask a clarifying question. Note the context: what was the setting, what prompted this memory, what other knowledge was assumed? The technical instruction is only part of what was communicated — the surrounding context, the speaker's relationship to the knowledge, and the associated memory are also documentation.

steps.instruction: Why is it important to honor requests for confidentiality, even if the information seems harmless? What happens to trust in the community if you do not?
steps.culturalContext: What not to share: some knowledge is private, family-specific, or ceremonially restricted. If someone tells you something and then says 'but don't put that in your notes,' you honor that. If someone describes a community tradition that they frame as private ('we don't usually talk about this to outsiders'), note the existence of the practice without describing its content. The Foxfire approach recognized that the community's willingness to share depended entirely on trust built over time. One breach of confidentiality could close off an elder's willingness to share — and word travels in small communities. Foxfire's longevity as a documentation project rested on the community's continued trust in the students and teachers doing the work.

steps.instruction: How does Foxfire's model of returning documentation to the community compare to how academic researchers often treat fieldwork data? What difference does the 'community first' principle make?
steps.culturalContext: Community ownership of community knowledge: the knowledge documented belongs to the community, not to the person who documented it. Foxfire's model was to give copies of everything back to the community, to credit interviewees by name, and to make the documentation accessible to community members first. The question of who owns community knowledge — and who benefits from its documentation — is central to both Appalachian and Indigenous cultural sovereignty debates. Foxfire's answer (the community owns it) was not universal in its era and is still not universal today. OCAP principles (Ownership, Control, Access, and Possession) in First Nations contexts formalize the same principle: data sovereignty belongs to the community whose knowledge is documented.
